_comment: Golden cases for the LIVE decomposition eval (app/evals/live_decompose.py). Each case is a set of dimension descriptors (each as its own single-dimension discovery report), run through the REAL production decomposition prompt+model. The verdict is derived from how the settled set turned out: MERGE = all the input descriptors folded into ONE settled axis; KEEP = they stayed as ≥2 distinct axes. Graded against `metadata.expected` by exact match (categorical → no judge tier; see docs/ai-evals.md 'Grader architecture'). `given.reports` is the list of discovery reports the decomposer settles (one descriptor each here for a focused case). Definitions are exact slices of real runs.
judge_background: Decomposition takes several dimension definitions that were each discovered separately (by parallel discovery workers) and decides whether they describe ONE concept that should be folded into a single axis (merge), or ≥2 genuinely distinct axes that should stay separate (keep). Judge from the definitions alone, pre-score: merge when they are the same concept differing only in wording/examples; keep when at least one measures a genuinely different thing.

metadata.note: Three discovery workers carved the same concept (professional health/safety competence) — decomposition should fold all three into one axis.
metadata.label_rationale: MERGE: all three definitions describe the same concept — professional health/safety competence (first-aid, CPR, nursing, occupational health) deployable for member wellbeing — differing only in wording and examples, not in what they measure. An applicant scores identically against all three, so they are one axis; the fold is correct. The clean non-contested MERGE for the decomposition pass (N carvings of one concept), judged from definitions alone, pre-score.
metadata.source: Rank run 6 (2026-07-16), decompose_audit settled axis health_safety_skill (source_keys health_safety_skill, health_and_safety_skill, health_and_safety_skills). The three definitions are exact slices of run 6's raw discovery reports (fan_out_audit).

reports.name: Health & safety skill
reports.definition: The household's professional health or safety expertise that could benefit co-op members directly (e.g. first aid, CPR, occupational health).

reports.name: Health and safety skill
reports.definition: Whether the household brings health, medical, or first-aid skills that could enhance the safety and wellbeing infrastructure of the co-op community.

reports.name: Health and safety skills
reports.definition: Professional health or safety competencies — nursing, first-aid training, CPR instruction — that the household could deploy to improve building safety and member wellbeing.

metadata.note: Capacity vs. intent — two adjacent-looking axes that measure different things; decomposition should keep them apart, not over-fold.
metadata.label_rationale: KEEP: capacity vs. intent — genuinely distinct axes. A is how much spare time the household actually has; B is how concretely they commit to co-op work in their own words. A household can be genuinely committed yet honestly acknowledge thin hours (high B, low A), or have open weekends but say little specific (high A, low B). Folding them would hide whether a promising applicant actually has the time to deliver. The KEEP flavour for decomposition — NOT over-folding when N carvings look adjacent but measure different things.
metadata.source: Rank run 7 (2026-07-16), decompose_audit — discretionary_time_availability and participation_commitment_explicitness kept distinct. Representative definitions are exact slices of run 7's raw discovery reports (fan_out_audit).

reports.name: Discretionary time availability
reports.definition: The household's self-reported availability of spare time for co-op commitments, net of outside obligations such as touring schedules, intensive hobbies, or other regular commitments.

reports.name: Participation commitment explicitness
reports.definition: How concretely and specifically the household commits to co-op work obligations — work-day attendance, committee seats, maintenance rotations — in their own words.

metadata.note: Five discovery workers each carved co-op financial administration under a slightly different name — decomposition should fold all five into one axis.
metadata.label_rationale: MERGE: all five definitions describe the same underlying competency — treasurer- or bookkeeping-level financial skill applicable to running a co-op (budgets, reconciliation, audit prep, record-keeping). They would score the same household identically, so they are one axis; the fold is correct. A second, larger clean MERGE for the pass (a distinct concept from the health/safety one), judged from definitions alone, pre-score.
metadata.source: Rank run 6 (2026-07-16), decompose_audit settled axis financial_admin_skill (source_keys financial_admin_skill, financial_management_skill, financial_administrative_skill, financial_governance_skill, financial_management_skills). Definitions are exact slices of run 6's raw discovery reports (fan_out_audit).

reports.name: Financial & Administrative Skill
reports.definition: Demonstrated ability to manage shared finances, budgets, audits, record-keeping, or governance administration relevant to running a co-op.

reports.name: Financial / Bookkeeping Skill
reports.definition: The depth of financial competence the household can contribute — from full treasurer/audit experience down to basic bookkeeping or no claimed financial skill.

reports.name: Financial / Bookkeeping Skill
reports.definition: Whether one or both adults can directly serve the co-op's financial administration — bookkeeping, budget reconciliation, audit preparation, or treasurer functions.

reports.name: Financial Governance & Treasurer-Level Skill
reports.definition: Demonstrated ability to manage a collective's finances: reading and preparing budgets, reconciling accounts, passing audits, and transferring financial knowledge to successors.

reports.name: Financial Management & Bookkeeping Skills
reports.definition: Whether the household can take on the co-op's financial administration — maintaining budgets, reconciling accounts, preparing for audits, and ensuring fiscal transparency — based on professional skill or demonstrated experience.

metadata.note: Overlap trap: both definitions name 'arboriculture', but interior trade skill and outdoor/grounds skill are genuinely distinct axes — decomposition should NOT over-fold on the shared example.
metadata.label_rationale: KEEP: the harder KEEP — two axes whose definitions literally share an example word (arboriculture) yet measure different things. A qualified arborist or horticulturalist scores high on grounds/outdoor skill but may have no interior building trade skill; a licensed electrician scores high on trade maintenance but may have no outdoor expertise. Folding them on the shared word would let one skill masquerade as the other. Tests that the pass reads the whole definition, not a keyword overlap.
metadata.source: Rank run 6 (2026-07-16), decompose_audit — trade_maintenance_skill and grounds_outdoor_skill settled as separate axes; run 6's decision text explicitly reasons them apart (arborist vs. electrician). Definitions are exact slices of run 6's settled report.

reports.name: Trade / Hands-On Maintenance Skills
reports.definition: Licensed or professionally practiced trade skills directly applicable to building upkeep — electrical, carpentry, masonry, mechanics, arboriculture, etc.

reports.name: Grounds, Gardening & Outdoor Maintenance Skill
reports.definition: Practical skill in maintaining shared outdoor spaces — gardening, composting, arboriculture, grounds safety — that reduce the co-op's need for outside contractors.
